hero.title: Glashapullagh
hero.subtitle: Blanket bog restoration in West Limerick. Reversing decades of drainage through comprehensive ecological engineering — rewetting peatland, rebuilding habitats, and securing carbon for generations to come.

items.title: Slow Carbon Loss
items.description: Preventing the oxidation of dry peat by raising water tables, slowing carbon loss to the atmosphere and turning degraded bog back into a carbon store.

items.title: Improve Habitats
items.description: Protecting and enhancing habitats for ground-nesting birds including Curlew, Snipe, and Hen Harrier through targeted vegetation and predator management.

items.title: Restore Peat-Forming Plants
items.description: Encouraging the return of Sphagnum mosses and native bog vegetation by raising the local water table and stabilising exposed peat surfaces.

items.title: Rewet the Landscape
items.description: Blocking historic drainage channels using timber dams, peat plugs, composite dams, stone dams, and coir logs to restore the bog's natural hydrology.

parallaxBreaks.title: A Landscape in Recovery
parallaxBreaks.text: The Glashapullagh site is a cutover blanket bog that had been historically drained, leading to severe peat compaction, the spread of scrub and rushes, and significant peat loss. A comprehensive Restoration Action Plan was developed using extensive drone and ground surveys to reverse this degradation.

parallaxBreaks.title: Recovery Takes Time
parallaxBreaks.text: But at Glashapullagh, it has begun. Rewetting is slowing carbon loss, habitats are improving for wildlife, and peat-forming plants are returning. The bog is healing — drain by drain, dam by dam, season by season.

about.label: About
about.title: The Glashapullagh Project
paragraphs: The Glashapullagh site is a cutover blanket bog in West Limerick that had been historically drained, leading to severe peat compaction, the spread of scrub and rushes, and significant peat loss. To reverse this degradation, a comprehensive Restoration Action Plan was developed using extensive drone and ground surveys to map vegetation, peat depth, slope, and drain dimensions.

Approved by the National Parks and Wildlife Service (NPWS), the restoration works at Glashapullagh were initiated with clear objectives: to slow carbon loss to the atmosphere by preventing the oxidation of dry peat; to improve habitats for wildlife, specifically protecting ground-nesting birds; and to encourage the return of peat-forming plants, specifically Sphagnum mosses, by raising the local water table.

The project serves as both a working restoration site and a model for blanket bog recovery across the Atlantic seaboard. Every technique deployed — from timber dams to geotextile stabilisation — has been carefully matched to the site's specific topographical and geological conditions.

topoMap.label: Location
topoMap.title: Topographical Overview
topoMap.description: Interactive map of the Glashapullagh restoration site in West Limerick

items.title: Water Management & Drain Blocking
items.description: Drains dug into the bog previously caused rapid water loss. To rewet the site and remove the drainage gradient, several damming techniques were deployed: timber dams in shallow drains, peat plugs in deep drains, composite wood-peat dams on sloping ground, stone dams where peat was thin, and coir logs to block gullies and raise the water table.

items.title: Peat Stabilisation & Reprofiling
items.description: Historic turf cutting left behind steep peat banks (hags) that rapidly dry out, crack, and release carbon. Steep faces were graded into gentle slopes to reduce air exposure and improve water retention. On highly exposed bare peat, biodegradable jute netting was secured with wooden pins to shield the surface, prevent erosion, and create optimal conditions for moss establishment.

items.title: Habitat & Vegetation Management
items.description: Self-sown Sitka spruce from nearby plantations were removed. Conifers draw water from the peat, cast heavy shade that limits Sphagnum growth, and provide perches for predators such as Grey Crows that threaten ground-nesting birds like Curlew, Snipe, and Hen Harrier. Seedlings were pulled by hand, while larger saplings were cut and left to safely decay.

items.title: Site Logistics & Waste Management
items.description: All operations were strictly planned to respect the fragile ground. Materials were stored close to the site and transported carefully to minimise surface damage. Legacy waste — including dumped turf-gathering plastic bags, tyres, pallets, and water tanks — was responsibly removed to protect wildlife and safeguard water quality.

items.title: Timber Dams
items.description: Untreated larch planks embedded deeply into shallow drains, with a small notch to direct water safely. Expected to last 8–10 years while vegetation fills the drains naturally.

items.title: Peat Plugs
items.description: Native peat dug from nearby borrow pits, packed firmly into large deep drains and topped with original surface vegetation. Used where a thick peat layer remains at the base.

items.title: Composite Wood-Peat Dams
items.description: Heavy larch planks reinforced with timber posts driven deep into the drain base, backed by compacted peat. Deployed in wide (1.5–2.5m) or deep (1m) drains on sloping ground.

items.title: Stone Dams
items.description: Non-calcareous stone (granite or sandstone) placed where little peat remained or where the drain reached the mineral layer, protecting the bog's delicate acidic water chemistry.

items.title: Coir Logs
items.description: Biodegradable rolls made from coconut husks used to block gullies, dam small drains, and act as bunds in rush-dominated areas to raise the water table and encourage moss growth.

items.title: Geotextiles
items.description: Biodegradable jute netting laid on exposed bare peat and secured with wooden pins. Shields peat from heavy rain, prevents erosion, and retains surface moisture for moss establishment.

turbaryRights.label: Context
turbaryRights.title: Turbary Rights & Peatland Restoration
turbaryRights.intro: Turbary rights — the customary and historical rights to cut turf on an area of bogland — complicate peatland restoration in Ireland by deeply intertwining environmental goals with cultural identity, economic survival, and historical land ownership.

items.title: Cultural & Historical Significance
items.text: The practice of bringing home the turf is a ritualised labour central to rural Irish identity, symbolising self-sufficiency and a traditional way of life. Because these rights often stem from the historical resettlement of confiscated lands, any state interference is highly emotive and deeply influenced by Ireland's post-colonial history.

items.title: Economic & Fuel Security
items.text: Turbary rights provide critical fuel security for heating rural homes. Many local turf contractors rely on peat extraction for their livelihood, selling turf within the community. Restricting these rights raises concerns over lost income, access to affordable energy, and depreciation of land values.

items.title: Conservation Tensions
items.text: When the government designated certain bogs as protected Special Areas of Conservation (SACs) and introduced bans on cutting, turf cutters felt their property rights were being unfairly dismissed by a science-first approach that disrespected their local knowledge.

items.title: Community Resistance
items.text: The conflict between state conservation mandates and local turbary rights has resulted in severe, organised resistance. Many turf cutters have strongly opposed compensation or relocation schemes, reflecting the deep emotional and cultural attachment to the right to cut turf.
